## Patient Initial Report

**Patient Details**
- Age: 42-year-old
- Sex: Male

**Presenting Complaint**
- Polyuria
- Polydipsia
- Blurred vision
- Fatigue for 2 weeks

**History**
- 10-year history of type 2 diabetes

**Vital Signs**
- Blood pressure: 140/90 mmHg

**Laboratory Values**
- Random blood sugar: 350 mg/dl
- HbA1c: 10.5%

**Treatment Initiated**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

## Prelim Diagnosis Report

### **1. Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic Syndrome (HHS)**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A serious complication of diabetes characterized by hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, and dehydration without significant ketoacidosis.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Patient's history of type 2 diabetes.
- Presenting symptoms: polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, and fatigue.
- Significantly elevated random blood sugar: 350 mg/dl.
- High HbA1c: 10.5%.
- Elevated blood pressure: 140/90 mmHg.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Intravenous fluids
- Insulin infusion
- Electrolyte replacement

### **2. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia due to insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Patient has a history of type 2 diabetes.
- Elevated HbA1c.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Dietary changes
- Exercise
- Oral hypoglycemic agents or insulin

### **3. Dehydration**
**📌 Disease Description:**
- A condition caused by excessive loss of body fluids.

**🧠 Clinical Reasoning:**
- Symptoms of polyuria and polydipsia.
- Elevated blood sugar levels indicating significant fluid loss.

**✅ Recommendations:**
- Intravenous fluids
- Oral rehydration solutions## Best Practices Report

### **🩺 Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic Syndrome (HHS)** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/1011](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/1011)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, weight loss, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, hypotension, and potentially shock. It's characterized by profound hyperglycemia (>600 mg/dL), hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), and volume depletion without significant ketoacidosis. HHS can be the first presentation of type 2 diabetes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/](https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Hyperosmolar_hyperglycaemic_state/)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a medical emergency involving serum hyperosmolality, hyperglycemia, and dehydration without ketoacidosis, resulting from a relative lack of insulin. Post-recovery, many children with type 2 diabetes and HHS may only temporarily require insulin, with many being effectively managed through diet and oral agents.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482142/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482142/)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a diabetes mellitus complication with a high mortality rate. Clinical outcomes depend on age, dehydration level, and comorbidities. Diagnosis includes plasma glucose >600 mg/dL, effective osmolarity >320 mOsm/L, and absence of significant ketoacidosis.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.childrensmercy.org/health-care-providers/evidence-based-practice/cpgs-cpms-and-eras-pathways/hyperglycemic-hyperosmolar-syndrome-hhs-care-process-model/](https://www.childrensmercy.org/health-care-providers/evidence-based-practice/cpgs-cpms-and-eras-pathways/hyperglycemic-hyperosmolar-syndrome-hhs-care-process-model/)
  - **📖 Summary:** Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Syndrome Clinical Pathways promote evidence-based, safe, high-value care through clinical recommendations and standard processes developed by multidisciplinary expert committees.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21147-hyperosmolar-hyperglycemic-state)
  - **📖 Summary:** HHS is a life-threatening diabetes complication, mainly in Type 2 diabetes, caused by prolonged high blood sugar, leading to severe dehydration and confusion. Stopping diabetes medications can result in high blood sugar and HHS. A possible complication of HHS treatment is a too-rapid drop in blood sugar levels.

### **🩺 Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus** [4 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/24](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-us/24)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetes diagnosis requires two confirmed values: FPG ≥126 mg/dL (≥7.0 mmol/L), plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) 2 hours after 75 g oral glucose, or HbA1c ≥6.5% (≥48 mmol/mol). Alternatively, a single random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) plus hyperglycemia symptoms can diagnose diabetes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/24](https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/24)
  - **📖 Summary:** Diabetes diagnosis requires two confirmed values: FPG ≥126 mg/dL (≥7.0 mmol/L), plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) 2 hours after 75 g oral glucose, or HbA1c ≥6.5% (≥48 mmol/mol). Alternatively, a single random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL (≥11.1 mmol/L) plus hyperglycemia symptoms can diagnose diabetes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://pro.aace.com/clinical-guidance/diabetes](https://pro.aace.com/clinical-guidance/diabetes)
  - **📖 Summary:** The AACE algorithm provides visual guidance for managing type 2 diabetes and related comorbidities, promoting educational, research, and clinical practice enhancements. It considers culturally appropriate language, health literacy, community partnerships, technology innovation, and clinical trial recruitment to improve patient outcomes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://diabetes.org/newsroom/american-diabetes-association-2023-standards-care-diabetes-guide-for-prevention-diagnosis-treatment-people-living-with-diabetes](https://diabetes.org/newsroom/american-diabetes-association-2023-standards-care-diabetes-guide-for-prevention-diagnosis-treatment-people-living-with-diabetes)
  - **📖 Summary:** The ADA's Standards of Care include updated guidelines for preventing, diagnosing, and treating diabetes, addressing type 1, type 2, and gestational diabetes. It focuses on strategies to prevent or delay type 2 diabetes, reduce complications, mitigate cardiovascular and renal risk, and improve health outcomes.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/type-2-diabetes/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20351199](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/type-2-diabetes/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20351199)
  - **📖 Summary:** If target blood sugar isn't maintained with diet and exercise, medications or insulin therapy may be prescribed. Additional medicines like blood pressure and cholesterol-lowering drugs, and low-dose aspirin may be prescribed to prevent heart and blood vessel disease. Annual wellness visits include diabetes screening and monitoring of risk factors.

### **🩺 Dehydration** [5 sources]
- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.emedicinehealth.com/what_is_the_best_way_to_cure_dehydration/article_em.htm](https://www.emedicinehealth.com/what_is_the_best_way_to_cure_dehydration/article_em.htm)
  - **📖 Summary:** Fluid intake is the best way to treat dehydration. Mild dehydration can be managed at home with water, sports drinks, oral rehydration solutions, diluted juices, decaffeinated tea, milk, breast milk, soups, and Jell-O and ice cream.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/dehydration/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20354092](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/dehydration/diagnosis-treatment/drc-20354092)
  - **📖 Summary:** The only effective treatment for dehydration is replacing lost fluids and electrolytes. The best approach depends on age, dehydration severity, and its cause. For infants and children dehydrated from diarrhea, vomiting, or fever, use over-the-counter oral rehydration solutions.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.verywellhealth.com/dehydration-treatment-8704072](https://www.verywellhealth.com/dehydration-treatment-8704072)
  - **📖 Summary:** Mild dehydration, causing symptoms like headache and dry mouth, is treatable with increased oral intake or oral rehydration therapy. Severe dehydration requires prompt medical treatment. Healthcare providers diagnose dehydration by assessing physical signs like dry skin and oral symptoms.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.webmd.com/a-to-z-guides/dehydration-in-adults-treatment](https://www.webmd.com/a-to-z-guides/dehydration-in-adults-treatment)
  - **📖 Summary:** Dehydration is usually easily treated at home by getting out of the heat and drinking plenty of liquids. It's best to drink before you feel thirsty.

- **🔗 Source:** [https://www.jamda.com/article/S1525-8610(08)00111-4/fulltext](https://www.jamda.com/article/S1525-8610(08)00111-4/fulltext)
  - **📖 Summary:** Dehydration is a poorly defined term, especially in frail elders. There's reasonable consensus on risk factors, but less agreement on what constitutes clinical dehydration and how to treat it, particularly in survey processes.
